request.question: Who created an American police procedural television series that starred the American actor Archie Kao as a regular?

sentences: Law & Order: Criminal Intent is an American police procedural television drama series set in New York City, where it was also primarily produced.
Created and produced by Dick Wolf and RenĂ© Balcer, the series premiered on September 30, 2001, as the third series in Wolf's successful "Law & Order" franchise.
"Criminal Intent" focuses on the investigations of the Major Case Squad in a fictionalized version of the New York City Police Department set in New York City's One Police Plaza.
In the style of the original "Law & Order", episodes are often "ripped from the headlines" or loosely based on a real crime that received media attention.
Chicago P.D. is an American police procedural drama television series created by Dick Wolf and Matt Olmstead as the second installment of Dick Wolf's "Chicago" franchise.
The series premiered on NBC as a mid-season replacement on January 8, 2014.
The show follows the uniformed patrol officers and the Intelligence Unit of the 21st District of the Chicago Police Department as they pursue the perpetrators of the city's major street offences.
The eighth season of "" premiered on the USA Network in the United States on April 19, 2009.
It consisted of sixteen episodes, and concluded on August 9, 2009.
The day following each episode's broadcast on television, they are made available to purchase and download from the iTunes Store. ""
is an American police procedural television series set and filmed in New York City.
It is the second spin-off of the long-running crime drama "Law & Order", and was created by Dick Wolf and RenĂ© Balcer.
"Law & Order: Criminal Intent" follows the New York City Police Department's Major Case Squad, which investigates high-profile murder cases.
Homicide: Life on the Street is an American police procedural television series chronicling the work of a fictional version of the Baltimore Police Department's Homicide Unit.
It ran for seven seasons (122 episodes) on NBC from January 31, 1993 to May 21, 1999, and was succeeded by "", which also acted as the "de facto" series finale.
The series was originally based on David Simon's book "".
Many of the characters and stories used throughout the show were based on events depicted in the book.
"Blue Bloods" is an American police procedural television series created by Robin Green and Mitchell Burgess.
It premiered on September 24, 2010 on CBS.
The series centers on the Reagans, a family of police officers in New York City.

Frank Reagan (Tom Selleck) is the police commissioner with three grown children working in law enforcement: the oldest, Danny (Donnie Wahlberg), a pragmatic detective; Erin (Bridget Moynahan), an assistant district attorney who prosecutes many of the offenders Danny apprehends; and Jamie (Will Estes), a Harvard Law School graduate and a patrol cop in the NYPD.
Frank's second son, Joseph, an NYPD detective, was killed in the line of duty while investigating a group of dirty cops nicknamed "The Blue Templar".
On March 25, 2016, the series was renewed for a seventh season, premiered on September 23, 2016.

CSI: NY (Crime Scene Investigation: New York, stylised as CSI: NY/) is an American police procedural television series that ran on CBS from September 22, 2004, to February 22, 2013, for a total of nine seasons and 197 original episodes.

The show follows the investigations of a team of NYPD forensic scientists and police officers identified as "Crime Scene Investigators" (instead of the actual title of "Crime Scene Unit Forensic Technicians" (CSU)) as they unveil the circumstances behind mysterious and unusual deaths, as well as other crimes.
The series is an indirect spin-off from the veteran series "" and a direct spin-off from "", during an episode in which several of the "CSI: NY" characters made their first appearances.
It is the third series in the "CSI" franchise.
, an American police procedural television series, premiered on September 20, 1999 on NBC.

Created by Dick Wolf, it is the first spin-off of "Law & Order" and follows the detectives of a fictionalized version of the New York City Police Department's Special Victims Unit, which investigates sexually based offenses.
"SVU" originally aired on Monday nights at 9pm/8c EST, but it was moved to Friday nights at 10pm/9c after the ninth episode.
Showrunner Robert Palm felt too disturbed by the subject matter and left after the season's conclusion.
"" is an American police procedural television series set and filmed in New York City.
It is the second spin-off of the long-running crime drama "Law & Order".

"Law & Order: Criminal Intent" follows a distinct division of the New York City Police Department: the Major Case Squad, which investigates high-profile murder cases, such as those involving VIPs, local government officials and employees, the financial industry, and the art world.

Unlike the other series in the "Law & Order" franchise "Law & Order: Criminal Intent" gives significant attention to the actions and motives of the criminals, rather than primarily focusing on the police investigation and trial prosecution.
Episodes do not usually contain trials, and often end in confessions rather than plea bargains or verdicts.
"Miami Vice" is an American police procedural television series which was broadcast for five seasons on the National Broadcasting Company (NBC) between 1984 and 1990.

Starring Philip Michael Thomas, Don Johnson, Edward James Olmos, Olivia Brown, Saundra Santiago, John Diehl and Michael Talbott, it focuses on the lives of two undercover Metro-Dade police officers, Ricardo Tubbs (Thomas) and James "Sonny" Crockett (Johnson).
The series was created by Anthony Yerkovich, with Michael Mann and Dick Wolf serving as executive producers.
Archie David Kao (born December 14, 1969) is an American actor.
He is best known to American audiences for series regulars roles on "Chicago P.D.", "Power Rangers Lost Galaxy" as well as long-running hit "".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1â€“4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) â€” base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1â€“2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1â€“12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1â€“4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991â€“92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification â€” in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions â€” do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1â€“3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Who created an American police procedural television series that starred the American actor Archie Kao as a regular?
hop1_docs: Archie Kao | Archie David Kao (born December 14, 1969) is an American actor.  He is best known to American audiences for series regulars roles on "Chicago P.D.", "Power Rangers Lost Galaxy" as well as long-running hit "".

The Chinese Detective | The Chinese Detective is a British television police procedural drama series, first transmitted by the BBC between 1981 and 1982.  The series was created by Ian Kennedy Martin, who had previously devised "The Sweeney" and "Juliet Bravo".  The series starred British Chinese actor David Yip as Detective Sergeant John Ho, the first Chinese lead actor in a British television drama series.  The series offered traditional police procedural storylines in a setting of occasional prejudice and distrust within the police force, and the prejudice displayed by those Ho encounters whilst doing his job.  Like many other television detectives of the time, Ho was something of a maverick, often using unorthodox methods to solve crimes.  The series was set in and around London's docklands, before redevelopment began in the 1980s.

Angie Tribeca | Angie Tribeca is an American comedy television series created by Steve and Nancy Carell.  The series, a satire of police procedural shows, stars Rashida Jones as the titular police detective Angie Tribeca.  It also stars Hayes MacArthur, Jere Burns, Deon Cole, and Andree Vermeulen in supporting roles.

Jay Seals | Jay Seals (born March 23, 1976) is an American actor, known for his recurring role in the television drama "Mad Men" on AMC from 2010 to 2015.  Some of his other credits include the 2012 American television police procedural fantasy drama "Awake", as a police tech, Nat, who is considered to be a "tech-savvy cop".  He also appeared in numerous other television series and films.

Monk (TV series) | Monk is an American comedy-drama detective mystery television series created by Andy Breckman and starring Tony Shalhoub as the eponymous character, Adrian Monk.  It originally ran from 2002 to 2009 and is primarily a police procedural series, and also exhibits comic and dramatic tones in its exploration of the main characters' personal lives.  The series was produced by Mandeville Films and Touchstone Television in association with Universal Television.

Hannah Britten | Hannah Britten is a fictional protagonist in the American police procedural drama television series "Awake".  She is portrayed by Laura Allen.  The character first appeared in "Pilot" and last appeared in the series finale "Turtles All the Way Down".  Hannah was created and designed by series creator and executive producer Kyle Killen after he created and developed the series "Lone Star" for the Fox network.

Erik Estrada | Henry Enrique "Erik" Estrada (born March 16, 1949) is an American actor, voice actor, and police officer known for his co-starring lead role in the police drama television series "CHiPs", which ran from 1977 to 1983.  He later became known for his work in Spanish-language "telenovelas", his appearances in reality television shows and infomercials and as a regular voice on the Adult Swim series "Sealab 2021" as well as the movie "Cool Cat Saves The Kids".

- missing_facts_or_bridge:

  - Confirmation that Chicago P.D. is the intended American police procedural referenced.

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. "Chicago P.D. (TV series) â€” Wikipedia" (or the Chicago P.D. main page) â€” to get creator(s) and show metadata.
  2. "Chicago P.D. creator" (search query) or the pages for likely creators (e.g., Dick Wolf; Matt Olmstead) â€” to confirm creator credits.
  3. "Archie Kao â€” full Wikipedia page" â€” to confirm his role and billing on Chicago P.D.
hop2_docs: Dick Wolf | Richard Anthony Wolf (born December 20, 1946) is an American television producer, best known as the creator and executive producer of the "Law & Order" franchise, which since 1990 has included six police/courtroom dramas and four international spinoffs, as well as a creator and executive producer of the "Chicago" franchise, which since 2012 has included four Chicago-based police/courtroom/fire/medical dramas.  Wolf has won numerous awards, including an Emmy Award and a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

Chicago Food Truck Festival | The Chicago Food Truck Fest is an annual gathering of gourmet food trucks in the South Loop of Chicago, IL during the summer.  The fest was select one of the best food truck fest in America.  The event debuted at U.S. Cellular Field in 2014.  In 2015, U.S. Sen. Mark Kirk of Illinois made a visit to the Chicago Food Truck Fest to urge Chicago lawmakers to lift restrictions on food trucks to allow them to better prosper.  Also, in 2015, WGN sat down with the event creator Alex Blackshire for an in depth discussion about the event and the food truck scene in Chicago.

Jason Bitner | Jason Bitner (born Glen Ellyn, Illinois) is an author and project producer currently living in Chicago, Illinois.  He is the co-creator of Found Magazine, a show-and-tell project celebrating found notes, letters and other ephemera; the creator of Cassette from My Ex, a storytelling project about love and mixtapes; and producer for the documentary movie "La Porte, Indiana", based on Bitner's book of the same title.  Bitner's work has been widely published and reviewed, and has been featured in print, web, radio, and television appearances including "The New York Times", "The Chicago Tribune", "The Boston Globe", National Public Radio, and "The New Yorker".

Kay Sumner | Kay Bachman Sumner is a television and film producer who was the producer and co-creator for the television series "The Dog Whisperer", for which she received Emmy Award nominations in 2006, 2007, and 2009.  Sumner was creator and producer of "Any Woman Can Fix It", a home improvement television series for women, played on CBC (Canada) for sixty-five shows run.  The show was eleven years in syndication.  She was creator and Producer for "Eye Bet" for CTV Network, and staff producer for ABC O&Oâ€™s, "Morning Show" and "The Anniversary Game" for WXYZ-TV, and the "Chicago Show" for WLS-TV.
Mike Kelley (writer) | Michael "Mike" Kelley (born 1967, Chicago, Illinois) is an American television writer and producer and creator of television series "Swingtown" and "Revenge".

John Schultz (writer) | John Schultz (July 28, 1932 â€“ May 6, 2017) was an American writer of fiction and non-fiction.  He was also a teacher of writing, the creator of the Story Workshop method of writing instruction, and an emeritus professor in the Fiction Writing Department at Columbia College Chicago.  He was born on July 28, 1932, in Iberia, Missouri, served as an army medic from 1953 to 1955, and then worked in Chicago as a writer and teacher.  He died in 2017 at the age of 84 in his home of Riverside, a suburb of Chicago.

Debra Hand | Debra Hand, creator of the nine foot tall bronze statue of Paul Laurence Dunbar commissioned by the Chicago Park District for Dunbar Park in Chicago, is a self-taught artist and sculptor whose work graces prominent museum collections.  In fact, it was at the prestigious DuSable Museum of African American History where Hand first publicly unveiled her work.
response.summary_2: Chicago P.D. â€” Dick Wolf is credited in the supplied passages as a creator and executive producer of the Chicago franchise, which includes Chicago P.D.; the provided passages do not confirm any additional co-creator(s).
